title: Age on Other Planets Calculator - Planetary Age Converter
tagline: Calculate how old you would be on Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, or Neptune based on each planet's orbital period around the Sun.
intro: Enter your age in Earth years and select a planet to discover your age in planetary years based on that planet's orbit.

error.invalid: Please enter a valid positive age in Earth years.

about.heading: About the age on other planets calculator
about.body:
A "year" is simply the time it takes a planet to complete one full orbit around the Sun. Earth's year is defined as 365.25 days — the time our planet takes to travel its elliptical path. Every other planet in our solar system has a different orbital period, ranging from just 88 days for Mercury to nearly 165 Earth years for Neptune. The age-on-other-planets calculator converts your age in Earth years into the equivalent number of orbits you would have completed on any other planet.

The concept is straightforward: planetary age = Earth age / orbital period in Earth years. For Mercury, whose year is only 0.2408 Earth years (about 88 days), a 30-year-old on Earth has completed roughly 30 / 0.241 ≈ 124.5 Mercurian years. Conversely, for Neptune, whose year spans 164.8 Earth years, a 30-year-old on Earth has not yet completed a single Neptunian year — they would be just 0.18 years old in Neptune time.

The four inner planets — Mercury, Venus, Earth, and Mars — have shorter orbital periods than Earth's neighbors farther out. Mercury orbits the Sun in just 87.97 days, making its year the shortest in the solar system. Venus takes 224.7 days (0.615 Earth years), so ages on Venus are always higher than on Earth — a 25-year-old is about 40.7 Venusian years old. Mars takes 686.97 days (1.881 Earth years), so Martian ages are always lower — that same 25-year-old would be just 13.3 Mars years old.

The giant planets have dramatically longer orbital periods. Jupiter takes 11.86 Earth years per orbit, so a 30-year-old has completed only about 2.5 Jovian years. Saturn's 29.45-year orbit means most people alive today have not yet completed their first Saturnian birthday. Uranus (84.02 years) and Neptune (164.79 years) have orbital periods so long that no human in recorded history has lived to see a single complete orbit from birth.

These calculations assume circular orbits for simplicity, though real planetary orbits are slightly elliptical. The orbital periods used are the sidereal periods — the time to complete exactly one orbit relative to the stars — rather than synodic periods, which are measured relative to Earth. The difference is small for most planets but becomes significant for Mercury and Venus.

Beyond being a fun way to think about your age, these calculations have practical scientific applications. Mission planners use orbital periods to schedule launch windows for interplanetary spacecraft. Mars missions, for example, use the roughly 26-month synodic period of Mars to identify optimal launch opportunities when Earth and Mars are favorably aligned. Astronomers use orbital periods to calculate gravitational perturbations and predict planetary positions centuries into the future.

The orbital periods used in this calculator are: Mercury 0.2408 years (87.97 days), Venus 0.6152 years (224.7 days), Mars 1.8808 years (686.97 days), Jupiter 11.8627 years (4,332.6 days), Saturn 29.4475 years (10,759 days), Uranus 84.0168 years (30,687 days), and Neptune 164.7913 years (60,182 days).

examples.heading: Age on other planets examples
examples.intro: Your Earth age converted to the number of planetary orbits completed on each world in our solar system.

items.note: Mars orbits the Sun every 1.881 Earth years. 30 / 1.881 = 15.95. You would have completed about 16 Martian orbits.
Venus orbits much faster (0.615 Earth years per orbit). 25 / 0.615 = 40.64. Venus ages accumulate quickly relative to Earth.
Jupiter's vast orbit takes 11.86 Earth years. 40 / 11.86 = 3.37. Even at 40 Earth years you would only be on your 4th Jovian year.
Mercury's year is only 88 days. 30 / 0.241 = 124.5. Mercury years accumulate extremely quickly from an Earth perspective.

howto.heading: How to use the age on other planets calculator
steps: Enter your current age in Earth years — you can use decimal values for greater precision (e.g. 30.5 for thirty and a half years).
Select a planet from the dropdown: Mercury, Venus, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, or Neptune.
Click Calculate Planetary Age to see how many orbits of the selected planet you would have completed at your current Earth age.
The result also shows the planet's orbital period in Earth years and Earth days, so you can understand the relationship between the two timescales.
Try different planets and ages using the preset example buttons to explore how dramatically orbital periods differ across the solar system.

faq.heading: Age on other planets FAQ
faq.q1: Why is your age on Mercury so much higher than on Earth?
faq.q1_a: Mercury completes an orbit of the Sun in just 87.97 Earth days — about 0.241 Earth years. Because a Mercury year is so short, you accumulate many more Mercury years than Earth years in the same amount of real time. A 30-year-old has completed approximately 124 Mercury orbits but only 30 Earth orbits.
faq.q2: Could you ever be older than 1 year old on Neptune?
faq.q2_a: Only if you live to be at least 164.79 Earth years old — Neptune's orbital period. Since no human has ever lived that long, no one born on Earth has ever completed a single Neptunian year. The oldest verified human, Jeanne Calment, died at 122 years old, reaching only 0.74 Neptune years.
faq.q3_a: Yes, for the purpose of converting Earth ages to planetary-orbit counts. The orbital periods used are the sidereal orbital periods from NASA data, accurate to five significant figures. The calculation assumes you were born on both planets simultaneously, which is a useful thought experiment even though humans cannot currently live on other planets.
faq.q4: What is the difference between a sidereal and synodic year?
faq.q4_a: A sidereal year is the time for a planet to complete one full orbit relative to the background stars — this is the "true" orbital period used in this calculator. A synodic year is the time between successive alignments of a planet with the Sun as seen from Earth, and it depends on both planets' orbital periods. For planning Mars missions, the ~26-month synodic period matters; for calculating your Martian age, the 1.881-year sidereal period is the correct value to use.
faq.q5: What would aging on Mars actually feel like?
faq.q5_a: Biologically, time passes at the same rate everywhere — your cells age the same way regardless of which planet you are on, because aging is driven by biochemistry, not orbital mechanics. The planetary age conversion is a fun mathematical exercise showing how many local years you would have celebrated. A Mars year (686.97 days) is about 1.88 Earth years, so you would only celebrate a Martian birthday every 1.88 years of real time.
faq.q6: Which planet's year is closest to Earth's?
faq.q6_a: Mars has the most similar orbital period to Earth, completing one orbit in 686.97 days (1.881 Earth years). This makes Martian ages the closest to Earth ages among the other planets. Venus is the next closest at 224.7 days (0.615 Earth years), though a Venus year is significantly shorter than Earth's, not longer.
